mission.title: Install and Configure Rancher / RKE2
mission.description: Rancher is a complete container management platform that simplifies deploying and managing Kubernetes clusters across various environments. It's particularly useful for organizations looking to streamline their container orchestration and management processes.

steps.title: Install Rancher Server
steps.description:
Run the Rancher server using Docker. Ensure Docker is installed on your Linux node. Execute the following command:
```bash
sudo docker run -d --restart=unless-stopped -p 80:80 -p 443:443 --privileged rancher/rancher:v2.10.0
```
After the command completes, access Rancher by visiting `https://localhost` in your web browser.

steps.title: Connect kubectl to Rancher
steps.description:
Once Rancher is running, you can configure `kubectl` to connect to your Rancher-managed clusters. First, get the kubeconfig file from Rancher:
1. Log in to the Rancher UI.
2. Navigate to the cluster you want to manage.
3. Click on 'Kubeconfig File' to download it.
4. Set the KUBECONFIG environment variable:
```bash
export KUBECONFIG=path/to/downloaded/kubeconfig.yaml
```
Verify the connection:
```bash
kubectl cluster-info
```

steps.title: Post-install Configuration
steps.description:
Configure monitoring and autoscaling for your clusters. For example, to enable the Horizontal Pod Autoscaler, you can use the following command:
```bash
kubectl autoscale deployment your-deployment-name --cpu-percent=50 --min=1 --max=10
```
Ensure that metrics-server is installed in your cluster for HPA to function correctly.

uninstall.title: Remove Rancher Server
uninstall.description:
To uninstall Rancher, stop and remove the Docker container:
```bash
sudo docker ps -a # to find the container ID
sudo docker stop <container_id>
sudo docker rm <container_id>
```
Additionally, clean up any persistent storage if configured.

upgrade.title: Upgrade Rancher Server
upgrade.description:
To upgrade Rancher, first back up your data. You can create a backup by exporting the database. Then, pull the new version and run it:
```bash
sudo docker pull rancher/rancher:v2.10.0
sudo docker run -d --restart=unless-stopped -p 80:80 -p 443:443 --privileged rancher/rancher:v2.10.0
```
Verify the upgrade by checking the Rancher UI and running:
```bash
kubectl get pods -n cattle-system
```
If issues arise, you can roll back to the previous version by running the older image.

troubleshooting.title: Rancher UI not accessible
troubleshooting.description:
If the Rancher UI is not accessible, check if the Docker container is running:
```bash
sudo docker ps
```
If it's not running, check the logs for errors:
```bash
sudo docker logs <container_id>
```
Common issues include port conflicts or insufficient resources.

troubleshooting.title: kubectl not connecting to Rancher
troubleshooting.description:
Ensure that the KUBECONFIG is set correctly. Run:
```bash
echo $KUBECONFIG
```
If it points to the wrong file, reset it. Also, verify that the kubeconfig file is valid and contains the correct cluster information.

troubleshooting.title: Pods in CrashLoopBackOff
troubleshooting.description:
If you see pods in a CrashLoopBackOff state, check the pod logs:
```bash
kubectl logs <pod_name>
```
Common causes include misconfigurations in deployments or insufficient resources allocated to the pods.

versionNotes.changes: Introduced support for Kubernetes 1.30, improved UI performance, and enhanced security features.
versionNotes.deprecations: Deprecated support for Kubernetes versions below 1.27.

versionNotes.changes: Added new monitoring capabilities and improved cluster management features.

resolution.summary: A successful setup of Rancher should allow you to access the Rancher UI at `https://localhost`, manage Kubernetes clusters, and utilize features like monitoring and autoscaling. Ensure that all components are running smoothly and that `kubectl` is configured correctly.

prerequisites.cloudCLI: optional cloud CLI tool (gcloud, aws, az, oci)
prerequisites.description: Ensure Docker is installed and running on your Linux node.
